section.title: Tola and Jair
section.sectionRationale: After the bloody self-destruction of Abimelech in chapter 9, the narrative breathes. Two judges, Tola and Jair, are given only a few quiet verses each: no battles are recorded, no speeches, only their tribe, their years, their sons, their towns, and their graves. These so-called minor judges show that God kept raising up deliverers for His people even in seasons without drama, and their calm brevity sets up, by contrast, the storm of Jephthah that follows.
section.thesis: Even after Abimelech's carnage, the LORD keeps providing deliverers for Israel, and the quiet, undramatic faithfulness of Tola and Jair testifies that God's care for His people runs on steadily through years that make no headlines.
section.frameNote: Two brief notices: Tola of Issachar who rose up to save Israel and judged twenty-three years, verses 1 to 2; and Jair the Gileadite with his thirty sons and thirty towns, who judged twenty-two years, verses 3 to 5.

units.label: Tola of Issachar
units.note: he rose up to save Israel

verses.text: After the time of Abimelech, a man of Issachar, Tola son of Puah, the son of Dodo, {{qum|rose up}} to {{yasha|save}} Israel. He lived in Shamir, in the hill country of Ephraim.
Tola {{shaphat|judged}} Israel twenty-three years, and when he died, he was buried in Shamir.
Tola was followed by Jair the Gileadite, who {{shaphat|judged}} Israel twenty-two years.
He had thirty sons who rode on thirty donkeys. And they had thirty towns in the land of Gilead, which to this day are called Havvoth-jair.
When Jair died, he was buried in Kamon.

qum.gloss: To rise up, arise, stand. Tola rose up to save Israel; the verb marks the moment God brings a deliverer onto the stage. Throughout Judges the raising up of a judge is God's own initiative, and the same word is later used of the prophet and the King God will raise up for His people.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue. Tola rose up to save Israel. It is the root behind the name Joshua and Jesus, the LORD saves; even these brief, obscure judges are cast as saviors, small shadows of the great Deliverer to come.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, govern, lead, deliver justice. The book of Judges is named for the shophet, the judge, who was less a courtroom official than a Spirit-raised deliverer and leader. Tola and Jair each judged Israel for over twenty years, keeping order and rest between the crises.

basic.context: Chapter 9 ended in horror: Abimelech, Gideon's son, murdered his brothers and grasped at kingship until he and the men of Shechem destroyed one another. Now the pace slows right down. Two judges are named, Tola of Issachar and Jair of Gilead, and we are told almost nothing about them: their families, how long they led, and where they were buried. No wars, no words, no failures are recorded. These are usually called the minor judges, not because they mattered less, but because their stories are short. Their quiet notices show that God kept on providing leaders to hold Israel together even in years that produced no dramatic rescue.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and these verses are deliberately spare. We should not read too much into the silence, nor invent adventures the text does not give. The point lies in the pattern: after chaos, God raises up a deliverer; life settles for a generation; then the notice of a death and a burial closes the account. The repeated shape teaches that God's faithfulness is not measured only by spectacular deliverances but also by long, unremarkable stretches of steady leadership.
basic.originalLanguages: Tola qum, rose up, to yasha, save, Israel; the verb save is the root behind the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD saves. Both men shaphat, judged, Israel, the very word that names the book, describing not a courtroom judge but a God-given deliverer and leader. Jair's thirty sons on thirty donkeys in thirty towns hint at wealth and influence, a settled, prosperous rule.
basic.typology: Every judge who rises up to save is a small, flawed picture of the true Savior. Tola, whose very role is to save Israel, faintly foreshadows the one whose name means the LORD saves, Jesus, who rises up not for a generation but forever. The quiet rest these judges secured points ahead to the lasting rest Christ gives His people.
basic.christ: These minor judges are cast as saviors of Israel, and the word for save is the root of the name Jesus. Where Tola and Jair rose up for a time and then were buried, Christ rose up to save, died, and rose again never to be buried, an unending Deliverer. Their brief, God-given leadership points to the King the whole book longs for, the righteous Savior who reigns forever.
basic.god: God is the quiet, faithful provider behind these bare verses. He is the one who raises up deliverers, even ones whose deeds are never recorded, so that His people are never left without care. He works not only in the headline rescues but through years of ordinary, steady leadership, showing a patient, providential kindness that keeps His people even when nothing dramatic is happening.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 2 gives the framework in which the LORD raised up judges to deliver His people. Acts 13 lists the judges in the story of God's dealings with Israel. And Hebrews 11 gathers many such deliverers, some barely named, as witnesses of faith, reminding us that God honors the obscure as well as the famous.
basic.oneStory: After Abimelech's ruin, God quietly raises up Tola and Jair to keep His people. Their brief, faithful years are one more link in the long chain of deliverers God provides, a chain that runs through all the judges and points beyond them to the one Savior who rises up to save and reigns forever.

context.literary: These verses form a hinge in Judges. They stand between the tragedy of Abimelech, chapter 9, and the crisis that brings Jephthah, chapter 10 verse 6 onward. Tola and Jair belong to a group scholars call the minor judges, given only short formulaic notices: name, tribe or town, years judged, notable details, death, and burial. Their brevity and calm deliberately contrast with the long, turbulent accounts around them, giving the reader a moment of rest and underscoring that God kept providing leaders even between the great deliverances.
context.historical: The period of the judges, roughly the centuries between Joshua and the monarchy, was a time of loose tribal life with no central king. A judge was a local or regional deliverer and leader, often raised in a crisis, whose influence could last decades. Jair's thirty sons riding thirty donkeys and holding thirty towns signals real wealth, status, and a broad ruling clan in Gilead, east of the Jordan; the towns called Havvoth-jair, the villages of Jair, preserved his name in the land for generations.
context.canonical: The minor judges fill out the picture of a nation repeatedly needing, and repeatedly given, deliverers. They belong to the same divine pattern set out in Judges 2:16-19, where the LORD raised up judges who saved Israel. The whole sequence exposes the need for a lasting, righteous king, a need the book keeps sounding and the rest of Scripture answers in David and finally in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record, in a deliberately brief and orderly way, that God continued to raise up deliverers for Israel after Abimelech, and to set a moment of calm, settled leadership against the surrounding accounts of crisis, so that the reader sees God's steady faithfulness across ordinary as well as dramatic times.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what these judges did and how long they led; it does not prescribe a program to imitate. What endures is the truth beneath the notices: God faithfully provides for His people, and quiet, unremarkable faithfulness matters in His sight even when no great deeds are recorded.

debates.question: Why does Scripture give the minor judges such short, undramatic notices?

views.label: They led in relatively peaceful times
views.case: The absence of recorded wars may mean their years were genuinely calmer, so the text simply notes their steady leadership without a crisis to narrate; the brevity reflects the quiet of the era, not a lesser importance.

views.label: The author is being selective for a purpose
views.case: Judges is shaped around the downward spiral of the nation, so the writer lingers on the accounts that make that point and compresses others; the minor judges are noted to show the continuity of God's provision while the spotlight stays on the larger theme.

debates.bearing: Either way, the brief notices affirm that God kept providing deliverers, and they teach us to value faithful, obscure service as well as the famous rescues.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary is small but weighty. Tola qum, rose up, the verb Judges uses when God brings a deliverer onto the stage; the raising up is God's own doing. He rose up to yasha, save, Israel, and that root, yasha, the LORD saves, stands behind the names Joshua and Jesus, so even these obscure men are cast as saviors. Both Tola and Jair shaphat, judged, Israel, the word that names the whole book and describes a God-given deliverer and leader rather than a courtroom official. Jair's thirty sons on thirty donkeys holding thirty towns quietly signals wealth, honor, and settled influence, and the place name Havvoth-jair, the villages of Jair, kept his memory alive in the land. The words themselves frame these brief lives as part of God's saving provision for His people.

typology.type: Tola who rose up to save Israel
typology.fulfillment: Christ, whose name means the LORD saves, who rises up to save His people not for a generation but forever, Matthew 1:21.

typology.type: The rest and settled years under the judges
typology.fulfillment: The lasting rest Christ gives to all who come to Him, Hebrews 4:9-10 and Matthew 11:28-29.
The one final Deliverer and King the book longs for, fulfilled in Jesus, the righteous Judge and Savior.

study.christ: The role given to Tola is striking: he rose up to save Israel, and the Hebrew for save is the very root of the name Jesus, the LORD saves. These minor judges are small, temporary saviors, raised up, ruling for a season, and then buried. Christ is the great and final Savior who rose up to save, was buried, and rose again never to die, an unending Deliverer for His people. The quiet rest their leadership secured for a generation points to the deeper rest Christ gives forever. And their very existence, one deliverer after another, exposes Israel's need for a lasting, righteous King, the need the whole book keeps pressing and that God answers in Jesus.
study.god: Behind these bare verses stands a faithful, providential God. He is the one who raises up deliverers, and He does so even when their deeds go unrecorded, so that His people are never abandoned. He works not only through the dramatic rescues that fill the famous chapters but through long, quiet years of steady leadership. In Tola and Jair we glimpse a God whose care for His people is patient and unbroken, holding them through ordinary seasons as surely as through crises, always providing what they need until the true Savior comes.

crossReferences.note: The LORD raised up judges who saved Israel from those who plundered them, the pattern in which Tola and Jair belong.
Paul recounts that God gave Israel judges until Samuel the prophet, placing these deliverers in the story of salvation.
The judges are gathered among the witnesses of faith, honoring deliverers whether famous or barely named.
You shall call His name Jesus, for He will save His people from their sins, the fullness of the saving role given to these judges.

study.oneStory: After the ruin of Abimelech, God does not leave His people leaderless; He quietly raises up Tola and Jair to save and to judge Israel for a generation. Their brief, faithful years are one more link in the long chain of deliverers God provides all through Judges. That chain, and the repeated need for it, points beyond every temporary judge to the one Savior who rises up to save, conquers death, and reigns as the righteous King forever.

quiz.q: What is said about Tola's role in Israel?

options.text: He rose up to save Israel and judged them twenty-three years.
options.note: Verses 1-2 describe Tola rising up to save and judging Israel for twenty-three years.

options.text: He judged Israel for many years in the hill country of Ephraim.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the key note that he rose up to save Israel.

options.text: He led Israel to a great victory in battle over Ammon.
options.note: No battle is recorded for Tola; that crisis comes later with Jephthah.

options.text: He seized power by force like Abimelech before him.
options.note: The opposite: Tola rose up to save Israel, unlike Abimelech who grasped at kingship.

quiz.q: What details are given about Jair the Gileadite?

options.text: He had thirty sons who rode thirty donkeys and held thirty towns, and he judged Israel twenty-two years.
options.note: Verses 3-4 record his sons, donkeys, towns, and years, signs of wealth and influence.

options.text: He was a prosperous leader in Gilead with a large family.
options.note: True in substance, but it leaves out the specific detail of his years and towns.

options.text: He built the city of Shamir and ruled from there.
options.note: Shamir was linked to Tola; Jair was buried in Kamon, and his towns were Havvoth-jair.

options.text: He refused to lead Israel and fled from his calling.
options.note: Nothing of the sort; Jair judged Israel for twenty-two years.

quiz.q: Why are Tola and Jair often called minor judges?

options.text: Because their accounts are brief, giving little more than their years, families, and burials.
options.note: The label reflects the short notices, not any lesser importance in God's provision.

options.text: Because they judged for only a very short time.
options.note: Misleading, since both judged for over twenty years; the brevity is in the text, not their tenure.

options.text: Because they were unfaithful and failed as leaders.
options.note: No failure is recorded; the notices are simply short, not critical.

options.text: Because they were never truly appointed by God.
options.note: Tola rose up to save Israel, and both are counted among the judges God provided.

quiz.q: What do these quiet notices teach about God?

options.text: That God keeps providing deliverers for His people even in undramatic times.
options.note: The pattern shows God's steady, faithful provision across ordinary years, not only crises.

options.text: That God cares only about the famous, dramatic rescues.
options.note: The reverse: these brief notices show God's care in quiet seasons too.

options.text: That God had abandoned Israel after Abimelech.
options.note: Far from abandonment, God raised up new deliverers right after the ruin of chapter 9.

options.text: That leaders no longer mattered to Israel's life.
options.note: The judges held Israel together; their leadership plainly mattered.

quiz.q: How does Tola's role point forward to Christ?

options.text: He rose up to save Israel, and the word for save is the root of the name Jesus, the LORD saves.
options.note: Tola is a small, temporary savior pointing to the great and final Savior, Jesus.

options.text: He rescued Israel from all their enemies forever.
options.note: His saving was real but temporary; only Christ saves finally and forever.

options.text: He was a sinless king who reigned without end.
options.note: Tola was a judge who died and was buried, not a sinless, eternal king; that is Christ alone.

options.text: He has no connection to the coming Savior.
options.note: His very role, to save Israel, foreshadows the Savior whose name means the LORD saves.

questions: Tola and Jair are barely described, yet God used them to keep His people. How does that comfort you if your own service feels obscure and unnoticed?
These judges rose up right after the chaos of Abimelech. Where have you seen God quietly provide steady leadership after a season of upheaval?
The text values long, unremarkable years of faithfulness as much as dramatic rescues. Are you tempted to think only the big, visible things count with God?
Tola rose up to save Israel, a faint shadow of Jesus, the LORD saves. How does knowing Christ is the true and lasting Savior steady you when human leaders come and go?
God never left His people without a deliverer. How can that assurance shape the way you trust Him in the ordinary stretches of your life right now?

translationNotes.note: Rose up to save translates a phrase that casts Tola as a deliverer; the verb for save, yasha, is the root behind the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD saves.
Judged renders shaphat; the BSB footnote notes it can also mean governed or led, describing a God-given leader and deliverer rather than only a courtroom judge, here and throughout the book.
Havvoth-jair means the villages, or tent-camps, of Jair; the BSB footnote gives the villages of Jair, a place name that preserved his memory in Gilead.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 10:1-5. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Ordinary hyphens retained in twenty-three, twenty-two, and Havvoth-jair. Sermon status none: no verified expository sermon on this specific movement located this session.
